rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is a benign, localised autoimmune dermatosis (presumptive cutaneous lichen planus) with severe symptomatic distress (intractable pruritus, two nights of sleep loss, self-inflicted excoriation) but NO systemic, airway, or established-infection features — afebrile, no spreading erythema or purulence. It is fully GP/telehealth-manageable: it needs prompt symptomatic treatment (potent topical steroid + nocturnal sedating antihistamine) to break the itch-scratch cycle and pre-empt secondary infection, plus GP review in 7-10 days. T2 (prompt primary-care management, effectively at this telehealth contact, within 24-48 hours) matches the need; it is neither routine-only (the acute distress and infection portal warrant promptness) nor urgent-care/ED (no red flag is currently active).

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Excoriated areas become hot, rapidly swollen, spread redness, or drain yellow pus (localised secondary bacterial infection)
escalation_edges.rationale: Localised secondary infection of the broken skin needs same-day clinical review and likely oral antibiotics; the open excoriations and living alone raise the stakes.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If any scratched spot becomes hot, puffy, spreads redness, or starts oozing yellow pus, see a GP or urgent care the same day — you may need an antibiotic.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, chills, or rapidly spreading redness with feeling unwell (spreading cellulitis / systemic infection)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to an emergency department without delay if these develop
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever or rapidly spreading cellulitis from a skin source signals a systemic infection needing emergency assessment and possibly intravenous antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a fever or chills, or the redness spreads quickly and you feel unwell, go to the emergency department — don't wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A similar rash, white lacy patches, sores or soreness appears inside the mouth or on the genitals (mucosal lichen planus)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Prompt GP review within a few days
escalation_edges.rationale: Mucosal involvement needs targeted therapy and, for the erosive oral form, longer-term specialist surveillance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you notice sores, white lacy patches or soreness in your mouth or on your genitals, book a GP review in the next few days so it can be treated properly.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one at home to notice deterioration of the scratched skin overnight
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Make the secondary-infection safety-net concrete and self-checkable (what to look for, when, and exactly where to go), and consider a slightly lower threshold for review given there is no second observer.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Start the strong cream on the spots and take the night-time antihistamine to settle the itch and help you sleep, keep your nails short and cover the treated skin with cotton sleeves at bed. Book a GP review in 7-10 days to check it is improving and to confirm the diagnosis. Watch the scratched areas daily.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Regular GP appointment within 7-10 days (or your telehealth service sooner if the itch is not controlled)
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If the scratched skin becomes hot, swollen or oozing pus, or you develop a fever after hours, use an after-hours GP or urgent care the same day; go to an emergency department if you feel systemically unwell with fever and spreading redness.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Presumptive cutaneous lichen planus with acute pruritic exacerbation and excoriation; commenced potent topical corticosteroid + nocturnal sedating antihistamine; for review of efficacy and consideration of dermoscopy/punch biopsy to confirm, and mucosal screening.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as pure self-care with no prescription treatment and no infection safety-net, leaving the open excoriations and severe itch unmanaged.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Untreated itch-scratch cycle progresses to secondary cellulitis in a person living alone, unnoticed until systemically unwell.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Routine-only GP review (1-2 weeks) without arranging prompt symptomatic treatment or an explicit infection safety-net.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a benign, afebrile, localised cutaneous dermatosis to the emergency department or calling an ambulance — misreading symptomatic acuity as an emergency.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot confirm lichen planus (no dermoscopy/biopsy) or definitively exclude scabies (no close burrow inspection).
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: State a presumptive diagnosis, treat symptomatically, arrange in-person GP/dermatology review for confirmation, and keep an explicit scabies/infection safety-net.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either asserting a confirmed remote diagnosis, or empirically treating for scabies/infection without evidence.
